name: 2017 Northern California Wildfire Claims
description: PG&E faced $7.5 billion in claims costs associated with the 2017 Northern California wildfires, requiring securitization and recovery bonds to finance.

name: 2019 Kincade Fire Liability
description: PG&E Corporation and the Utility could be liable as a result of the 2019 Kincade fire, with probable losses recorded at the lower end of the reasonably estimable range.

name: 2020 Zogg Fire Liability
description: Criminal charges have been filed against the Utility in connection with the 2020 Zogg fire, and if convicted, total losses could materially exceed accrued estimated liabilities, with full restitution to victims required under California law.

name: 2021 Dixie Fire Liability
description: The Utility has recorded receivables of $503 million for regulatory recovery of costs related to the 2021 Dixie fire.

name: 2022 Mosquito Fire Liability
description: The Utility has recorded receivables of $60 million for regulatory recovery of costs related to the 2022 Mosquito fire.

name: A-to-z Guarantee Cost Risk
description: As third-party seller sales grow, the cost of the A-to-z Guarantee program reimbursing customers will increase and could negatively affect operating results.

name: AI Cloud Services Risk
description: NVIDIA AI cloud services may not be successful and will take time, resources and investment. New business models or strategies may not be successful and the company may fail to sell any meaningful standalone software or as-a-service solutions.

name: AI Data Usage Regulatory Risk
description: Regulatory developments may constrain the ability to use data for AI services and insights, impeding expected returns on investment and affecting customer perceptions of quality and operational efficiency.

name: AI Development and Use Risk
description: AI algorithms or training methodologies may be flawed, datasets may be biased, and content generated by AI may be offensive or harmful, resulting in reputational or competitive harm or liability.

name: AI Ethics and Regulation Risk
description: Risk of brand or reputational harm, competitive harm or legal liability relating to the responsible development and use of AI models and systems. Compliance with government regulation in AI ethics may increase costs, and changes in AI-related regulation could disproportionately impact the company and require changes to business practices.

name: AI Harm and Liability
description: Content generated by AI systems may be offensive, illegal, or otherwise harmful. Ineffective or inadequate AI development or deployment practices could result in incidents that impair the acceptance of AI solutions or cause harm to individuals, customers, or society, or result in products and services not working as intended.

name: AI Market Competition
description: AI technology and services are expected to be a highly competitive and rapidly evolving market. Significant development and operational costs are required to build and support AI capabilities, products, and services.

name: AI Regulation Risk
description: Several states and localities have enacted measures related to the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning in products and services. A proposed European regulation on AI could impose onerous obligations and require changes to business practices.

name: AI Regulatory Restriction Risk
description: The increasing focus on the strategic importance of AI technologies has resulted in regulatory restrictions targeting products and services capable of enabling or facilitating AI, including potential additional unilateral or multilateral export controls on AI technologies such as GPUs and associated products.

name: AI Regulatory and Legal Liability
description: Implementation of AI systems could subject Microsoft to competitive harm, regulatory action, legal liability including under new proposed legislation regulating AI in jurisdictions such as the EU, and brand or reputational harm.

name: AWS Revenue Growth Impact
description: The current macroeconomic environment and enterprise customer cost optimization efforts are expected to negatively impact AWS revenue growth rates.

name: Abuse of Platforms
description: For platform products and services that provide content or host ads influenced by third parties, reputation or user engagement may be negatively impacted by abuse of platforms including advertising, professional, marketplace, and gaming platform abuses.

name: Account Holder Default Risk
description: There is a risk that account holders who use credit products will default on their payment obligations, potentially resulting in charge-offs or negatively impacting revenue share arrangements. Non-payment rates may increase due to worsening economic conditions, rising interest rates, or high unemployment.

name: Acquisition Financing Risk
description: Potential issuances of debt to finance acquisitions, resulting in increased debt, increased interest expense, and compliance with debt covenants or other restrictions.

name: Acquisition Integration Risk
description: Cultural challenges associated with integrating employees from the acquired company into our organization, and retention of employees from the businesses we acquire.

name: Acquisition Regulatory Risk
description: Acquisitions may be subject to government regulatory reviews that can prevent their completion and result in material costs, as demonstrated by the termination of the Arm Limited acquisition.

name: Acquisition Termination Risk
description: NVIDIA wrote off a $1.35 billion prepayment related to the termination of the Arm acquisition transaction in FY2023.

name: Acquisition and Investment Risk
description: The company faces risks from its inability to realize the expected benefits of business investments or acquisitions, including integration challenges, regulatory hurdles, talent retention difficulties, technology evolution, and financial impacts such as write-offs and impairment charges.

name: Acquisition and Merger Risks
description: Future acquisitions or mergers may require the company to issue additional equity securities, incur debt, or recognize contingent liabilities and amortization expenses related to intangible assets, which could reduce profitability.

name: Acquisitions and Strategic Alliances Risk
description: Acquisitions, joint ventures, and strategic alliances involve significant challenges and risks including failure to advance business strategy, unsatisfactory return on investment, compliance obligations, difficulty integrating employees and technology, and management distraction.

name: Additional Tax Liabilities and Collection Obligations
description: The company is subject to a variety of taxes and tax collection obligations in the U.S. and numerous foreign jurisdictions, and may recognize additional tax expense due to changes in laws, regulations, and administrative practices, including new revenue-based taxes targeting online commerce.

name: Adverse Economic Conditions
description: Economic and industry uncertainty, including recession, inflation, changes in fiscal, monetary, and trade policy, currency fluctuations, higher interest rates, tighter credit, and lower consumer confidence, can adversely affect business and financial results through increased supply chain costs, reduced margins, and decreased demand.

name: Aging Infrastructure Risk
description: The Utility's aging infrastructure poses risks to safety and system reliability, increasing the likelihood of catastrophic wildfires.

name: Air Quality and Climate Change Regulation
description: The Utility's electric generation plants, natural gas pipeline operations, vehicle fleet, and fuel storage tanks are subject to numerous air pollution control laws including the federal Clean Air Act and state and local statutes covering GHG emissions, CO2, SO2, NOx, particulate matter, and other pollutants.

name: Annual Rate Update Protests
description: Parties have protested the Utility's annual formula rate updates submitted each December, and these protests are pending before the FERC.

name: Anti-Corruption Risk
description: PayPal is subject to anti-corruption laws including the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act and the U.K. Bribery Act, which prohibit offering or accepting anything of value to influence official action or gain an unfair business advantage.

name: Anti-Corruption and Trade Law Risk
description: The FCPA and other anti-corruption laws prohibit corrupt payments by employees, vendors, or agents. Failure to comply could result in significant fines, penalties, criminal sanctions, prohibitions on business conduct, and reputational damage.

name: Anti-Money Laundering Compliance Risk
description: Non-compliance with anti-money laundering laws and regulations or economic and trade sanctions may subject PayPal to significant fines, penalties, lawsuits, and enforcement actions, result in regulatory sanctions and additional compliance requirements, increase regulatory scrutiny, restrict operations, and damage reputation and brands.

name: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Risk
description: Regulators globally continue to increase standards regarding anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing, potentially requiring revision or expansion of compliance programs and making compliance more costly and operationally difficult.

name: Anti-Money Laundering and Sanctions Risk
description: PayPal is subject to anti-money laundering laws and regulations in the U.S. and other jurisdictions, as well as laws designed to prevent the use of financial systems to facilitate terrorist activities, and non-compliance could affect its ability to operate.

name: Antitrust Investigation Risk
description: The Company is subject to antitrust investigations in various jurisdictions, including Europe, relating to App Store terms and conditions. Adverse findings could result in significant fines and required business changes, materially adversely affecting the Company's business, results of operations, and financial condition.

name: App Store Regulatory Risk
description: The Company faces risks from legislative initiatives impacting the App Store, such as the EU Digital Markets Act, which requires compliance by March 2024, and similar laws in other jurisdictions. These changes may affect developer charges, app distribution, and consumer communications.

name: Asset Retirement Obligation Estimation Risk
description: Changes in estimates and assumptions about future decommissioning costs, inflation rates, and decommissioning dates could materially affect the recorded asset retirement obligation.

name: Authentication Requirements Risk
description: Financial services regulators in various jurisdictions, including the U.S. and EU, have implemented authentication requirements for banks and payment processors intended to reduce online fraud, which could impose significant costs, make it more difficult for new customers to open PayPal accounts, and reduce the ease of use of products.

name: Banking Regulation Risk
description: PayPal must comply with European banking rules and regulations covering capitalization, funds management, corporate governance, anti-money laundering, disclosure, reporting, and inspection, and may also be subject to indirect regulation through partner financial institutions.

name: Brexit Risk
description: The U.K.'s departure from the EU could harm PayPal's business, financial condition, and results of operations. Brexit may contribute to instability in financial, stock, and foreign currency exchange markets, including volatility in the value of the British Pound and Euro.

name: Business Disruptions
description: Worldwide operations could be disrupted by natural disasters, extreme weather, power or water shortages, telecommunications failures, cloud service provider outages, terrorist attacks, political unrest, acts of war, epidemics, or other catastrophic events, leading to a decline in revenue and increased costs.

name: Business Interruption
description: Operations and facilities are subject to the risk of interruption by fire, power shortages, nuclear power plant accidents, terrorist attacks, ransomware and cybersecurity attacks, labor disputes, public health issues including pandemics such as COVID-19, and other events beyond the Company's control.

name: Business Interruptions and Systems Failures
description: Systems and operations may experience business interruptions or degradation of service due to DDoS attacks, insider threats, hardware and software defects, human error, natural disasters, public health crises, power losses, telecommunications disruptions, fraud, military or political conflicts, terrorist attacks, malware, or climate change-related events.

name: Business Interruptions or Systems Failures
description: Business interruptions or systems failures may impair the availability of the Company's websites, applications, products, or services, and could otherwise harm its business.

name: Business Model Competition
description: Companies compete based on a growing variety of business models including free applications, open source software, and advertising-funded services that directly compete with revenue-generating products.

name: Business Processes and Information Systems Disruption
description: Dependence on internal processes and information systems; modification or interruption of such systems may disrupt business, processes, and internal controls.

name: CCPA/CPRA Compliance Risk
description: The California Consumer Privacy Act and California Privacy Rights Act impose obligations including rights to access, delete and opt-out of sharing personal information, with statutory fines of up to $7,500 per violation and private right of action for data breaches.

name: COVID-19 Cost Recovery Risk
description: Costs incurred to implement the CPUC's Emergency Authorization and Order Directing Utilities to Implement Emergency Customer Protections during the COVID-19 pandemic were tracked in regulatory accounts and require CPUC authorization before they can be recovered through rates.

name: COVID-19 Pandemic Impact
description: Reduction in originations in merchant loans and advances in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

name: COVID-19 Pandemic Uncertainty
description: Uncertainty regarding the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic introduces risk to business results and operations.

name: COVID-19 Risk
description: Restrictions may be reimposed as the pandemic resurfaces, including lockdowns in China. End customer sales in China have been negatively impacted by lockdowns. COVID-19-related disruptions may continue to create supply chain and logistics constraints.

name: COVID-19 and Health Issues Risk
description: Sudden or sustained government lockdowns or actions to control case spread of COVID-19 or other global or local health issues impacting demand for products.

name: CPUC Penalties and Citations
description: The CPUC has delegated authority to the SED to issue citations and impose penalties for violations identified through audits, investigations, or self-reports, with a maximum statutory penalty of $100,000 per day and an administrative limit of $8 million per citation.

name: CPUC Penalty Risk
description: The CPUC can impose penalties of up to $100,000 per day, per violation for safety requirement violations pertaining to the design, construction, testing, operation, and maintenance of utility gas and electric facilities.

name: CPUC Ratemaking Mechanism Risk
description: The CPUC may discontinue or change ratemaking mechanisms in the future, or the Utility may incur environmental costs that exceed amounts the CPUC has authorized the Utility to recover through rates.

name: CPUC Revenue Authorization Risk
description: The CPUC may authorize lower revenues than the Utility requested or extend the recovery period, impairing the Utility's ability to timely recover operating costs and potentially slowing growth in rate base and earnings.

name: Capital Expenditure Disallowance Risk
description: Capital expenditures may be disallowed for ratemaking purposes, requiring recognition of a loss if the disallowance is probable and estimable.

name: Capital Expenditure Execution Risk
description: The completion of capital projects, timing of expenditures, and associated cost recovery may be affected by permitting requirements and delays, construction schedules, and the availability of labor, equipment, materials, and financing.

name: Capital Market Access Risk
description: If the Utility were unable to access the capital markets or the cost of financing were to substantially increase, its financial condition, results of operations, liquidity, and cash flows could be materially affected.

name: Capital Structure Compliance Risk
description: The Utility relies on equity contributions and long-term debt issuances to maintain its CPUC-authorized long-term capital structure of 52% equity and 48% debt and preferred stock, with a temporary five-year waiver granted from compliance with authorized capital structure upon emergence from Chapter 11.

name: Carbon Cost Exposure for Natural Gas Customers
description: Natural gas end-use customers face increasing exposure to carbon costs under CARB's cap-and-trade program through 2030, and potentially aggressive energy efficiency requirements could further reduce natural gas end use.

name: Carbon Neutrality Regulatory Risk
description: AB 1279 codified a statewide goal to achieve economy-wide carbon neutrality by 2045, and the Utility will need to participate in regulatory proceedings to determine how the state will achieve this target.

name: Carrier and Reseller Dependency Risk
description: The Company depends on the performance of carriers, wholesalers, retailers and other resellers, many of which also distribute products and services from competitors. There is no assurance that financing or subsidies offered by carriers will continue.

name: Catastrophic Event Costs
description: Incremental costs of responding to catastrophic events declared a disaster or state of emergency are recovered through California Energy Management Assistance applications.

name: Catastrophic Events
description: A disruption or failure of systems or operations because of a major earthquake, weather event, cyberattack, terrorist attack, pandemic, or other catastrophic event could cause delays in completing sales, providing services, or performing other critical functions.

name: Catastrophic Events and Emergency Costs
description: Costs associated with catastrophic events such as fires and storms declared emergencies require California Energy Management Assistance applications for recovery, which are subject to regulatory approval and uncertainty.

name: Change in Control Risk
description: Delaware law and the company's certificate of incorporation, bylaws, and agreement with Microsoft contain anti-takeover provisions that may discourage, delay, or prevent a change in control.

name: Change of Control Risk
description: Under the agreement with Microsoft for the Xbox, if someone makes an offer to purchase at least 30% of outstanding common stock, Microsoft may have first and last rights of refusal to purchase the stock, which could delay or prevent a change in control of NVIDIA.

name: Changes in Financial Accounting Standards
description: Changes in financial accounting standards or interpretations of existing standards could affect results of operations.

name: Changing Environmental Laws and Regulations
description: Environmental remediation costs could increase in the future as a result of new legislation, stricter standards, or more expansive application of existing environmental regulations. Failure to comply could expose the Utility to claims or civil and criminal fines.

name: Channel Inventory Risk
description: Undershipping GPU supply to channel partners to lower inventory levels; excess inventory with OEM partners due to rapid change in economic conditions.

name: Channel Partner Dependency Risk
description: NVIDIA sells most products through channel partners who sell to distributors, retailers, and end customers. Decisions made by these intermediaries can impair the ability to properly forecast demand.

name: China Data Localization Requirements
description: China law imposes various requirements relating to data processing and data localization. Data broadly defined as important under China law may not be transferable outside of China without prior assessment and approval by the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC).

name: China Export Restrictions on Semiconductors
description: During the third quarter of fiscal year 2023, the U.S. government announced new export restrictions and licensing requirements targeting China's semiconductor and supercomputing industries, affecting exports of certain chips, software, hardware, equipment, and technology to China (including Hong Kong and Macau) and Russia.

name: China Market Risk
description: Results and competitive position may be harmed if customers in China do not want to purchase alternative product offerings, if customers purchase from competitors, if customers develop internal solutions, or if USG does not grant licenses in a timely manner or denies licenses to significant customers.

name: China Operations Transition Risk
description: NVIDIA is required to transition certain operations out of China (including Hong Kong), which could be costly and time consuming, adversely affecting research and development, supply and distribution operations, and revenue.

name: Chinese Government Restrictions on Gaming
description: Restrictions imposed by the Chinese government on the duration of gaming activities and access to games may adversely affect Gaming revenue, and increased oversight of digital platform companies may adversely affect Data Center revenue.

name: Claims and Lawsuits Risk
description: The company is subject to a variety of claims and lawsuits arising from business practices, including major product releases, AI services, significant business transactions, warranty or product claims, employment practices, and regulation. Adverse outcomes may result in significant monetary damages or injunctive relief.

name: Claims, Litigation, and Government Investigations
description: Amazon is regularly subject to actual and threatened claims, litigation, reviews, investigations, and other proceedings involving patent and intellectual property matters, taxes, labor and employment, competition and antitrust, privacy, data use, data protection, data security, consumer protection, and other matters.

name: Climate Change - Temperature Impact
description: Rising ambient temperatures pose risks to equipment performance and influence electricity demand through increased air conditioning usage and the adoption of beneficial electrification technologies.

name: Climate Change Impact
description: Climate change may have an increasingly adverse impact on the business and those of customers, partners and vendors. Water and energy availability, extreme weather events, wildfires, and climate-related political instability may disrupt operations, cause higher attrition, losses and costs.

name: Climate Change Infrastructure Investment
description: The Utility will need to make substantial, sustained investments to its infrastructure to adapt to climate change, placing upward pressure on customer rates.

name: Climate Change Operational Risk
description: Climate change impacts on infrastructure resilience, requiring updates to design and construction standards for equipment and facilities to address current and future extreme weather conditions.

name: Climate Change Risk
description: The company faces risks related to climate change impacts and manages these risks through a science-based approach to reducing its climate change footprint.

name: Climate Regulatory and Compliance Risks
description: Current and emerging legal and regulatory requirements related to climate change, such as carbon pricing and ESG disclosure requirements, may result in increased compliance burdens, higher operating costs, and disruptions to PayPal's operations.

name: Climate-Related Laws and Regulations
description: The company's business may be subject to climate-related laws, regulations, and lawsuits, including carbon taxes, fuel or energy taxes, and pollution limits, resulting in greater direct and indirect costs, increased capital expenditures, and higher compliance and energy costs.

name: Climate-Related Physical Risks
description: PayPal's primary locations, particularly its headquarters in California, may be vulnerable to adverse effects of climate change, including drought, water scarcity, flooding, heat waves, wildfires, resultant air quality impacts, and power shutoffs associated with wildfire prevention.

name: Cloud Execution and Competitive Risk
description: Competitors are rapidly developing and deploying cloud-based services. Pricing and delivery models are evolving. Devices and form factors influence cloud service adoption. The company may not establish sufficient market share to achieve scale necessary to meet business objectives.

name: Cloud Infrastructure Cost Risk
description: Costs to build and maintain infrastructure to support cloud computing services will reduce previously achieved operating margins. Failure to generate sufficient usage of new products and services may negatively impact gross margins and operating income.

name: Cloud-Based Services Execution Risk
description: Increasing focus on cloud-based services presents execution and competitive risks as a growing part of the business involves cloud-based services available across the spectrum of computing devices.

name: Coastal and Flooding Risk
description: The Utility's assets located on the coast and near watersheds face increasing exposure to coastal, riverine, and pluvial flooding driven by climate-related changes in precipitation and sea level rise. The risk of damage to substations and operational interruptions is predicted to increase over time.

name: Collective Bargaining Agreement Expiry
description: Collective bargaining agreements in effect for the IBEW Local 1245, ESC Local 20, and SEIU will expire on December 31, 2025, posing potential labor negotiation risks.

name: Commercial Agreements and Strategic Alliances Risk
description: The company enters into commercial agreements covering web services, fulfillment, customer service, inventory management, payment processing, hardware, content, and third-party software, among other areas. Compensation may be lower than expected, agreements may be terminated if partner offerings are unsuccessful, and the company may be unable to renew or replace agreements on comparable terms.

name: Commodity Price Risk
description: The Utility is exposed to commodity price risk from its electricity and natural gas procurement activities, including procurement of natural gas and nuclear fuel for electricity generation, with price fluctuations potentially impacting cash flows.

name: Common Plant Allocation Risk
description: The common plant allocation decision in the TO18 proceeding will also be incorporated into TO19 and TO20 rate cases, creating uncertainty.

name: Competition Law Investigations
description: Amazon faces open investigations based on claims that aspects of its operations violate competition rules, including aspects of Amazon's U.S. and European marketplace for sellers, particularly with respect to use of data, fulfillment services, and featured offers.

name: Competition Law Risk
description: Competition law regulatory actions and court decisions may result in fines or hinder the ability to provide software benefits to consumers and businesses, reducing product attractiveness and revenue. Such actions could also cause withdrawal or modification of products, decrease asset value, or inhibit acquisitions.

name: Competition Risk
description: Competition in current and target markets could cause NVIDIA to lose market share and revenue. Competitors may offer cheaper products, better functionality, longer operating histories, larger customer bases, more comprehensive intellectual property portfolios, and greater financial resources.

name: Competition for Qualified Personnel
description: Competition for qualified personnel is intense, making human capital management a risk factor for the company.

name: Competition from Alternative Computational Approaches
description: NVIDIA faces competition from alternative computational approaches that may match its performance in target markets including Data Center, Gaming, Professional Visualization, and Automotive.

name: Competition from Alternative Providers
description: Customers can obtain electricity from alternative providers such as municipalities or CCAs, as well as from self-generation resources such as rooftop solar installations.

name: Competition from Energy Service Providers (DA)
description: California law allows qualifying non-residential electric customers of IOUs to purchase electricity from energy service providers rather than from the utilities under the Direct Access (DA) arrangement.

name: Competition in Cloud and Business Solutions
description: Dynamics competes with cloud-based and on-premises business solution providers such as Oracle, Salesforce, and SAP.

name: Competition in Email and Information Security
description: Security solutions spanning email security, information protection, identity, and governance face competition from web-based offerings and individual applications.

name: Competition in Markets
description: Competition in our current and target markets could cause us to lose market share and revenue.

name: Competition in Professional Networks
description: LinkedIn faces competition from online professional networks, recruiting and talent management companies, larger enterprises focused on human resource services, job boards, traditional recruiting firms, and providers of learning and development products and services.

name: Competition in Server Chips
description: NVIDIA's Grace CPU is designed to offer higher performance, twice the memory bandwidth, and improved energy efficiency compared to leading server chips, reflecting the competitive pressure in the server chip market.

name: Competitive Disadvantage from Export Controls
description: Export controls targeting GPUs and semiconductors associated with AI would restrict NVIDIA's ability to export technology, products, or services even though competitors may not be subject to similar restrictions, creating a competitive disadvantage.

name: Competitive Pressure in AI
description: Microsoft AI offerings compete with AI products from hyperscalers such as Amazon Bedrock, Amazon CodeWhisperer, and Google AI, as well as emerging competitors including Meta's LLaMA2 and open source solutions.

name: Competitive Pressure in Cloud
description: Azure faces diverse competition in the cloud computing market from companies such as Amazon, Google, IBM, Oracle, and VMware, as well as open source offerings.

name: Competitive Pressure in Database and BI
description: Microsoft database, business intelligence, and data warehousing solutions compete with products from IBM, Oracle, SAP, Snowflake, and other companies.

name: Competitive Pressure in Server Products
description: Microsoft server products face competition from a wide variety of server operating systems and applications, including Unix offerings from Hewlett-Packard, IBM, and Oracle, as well as Linux-based solutions from many vendors.

name: Competitive Technology Risk
description: Competing technologies and competitor product releases and announcements impacting demand for products.

name: Competitor Resource Advantage
description: Some current and potential competitors have greater resources, longer histories, larger customer bases, and greater brand recognition, enabling them to secure better supplier terms, adopt more aggressive pricing, and devote more resources to technology, infrastructure, and marketing.

name: Complex Laws, Rules, and Regulations
description: Operations could be affected by complex laws, rules and regulations including IP ownership, taxes, import/export requirements, anti-corruption, data privacy, competition, cybersecurity, environmental health and safety, AI, climate change, cryptocurrency, and consumer laws. Compliance can be onerous and expensive and could impact competitive position.

name: Complex and Changing Laws Across Markets
description: Business is subject to complex and changing laws, rules, regulations, policies, and legal interpretations governing banking, credit, deposit taking, money transmission, prepaid access, foreign currency exchange, privacy, data protection, cybersecurity, cryptocurrency, payment services, fraud detection, consumer protection, antitrust, economic sanctions, anti-money laundering, and counter-terrorist financing.

name: Component Availability Risk
description: The continued availability of custom components at acceptable prices can be affected if suppliers decide to concentrate on the production of common components instead of components customized to meet the Company's requirements.

name: Component Shortage
description: Components used by the Company, including those available from multiple sources, are at times subject to industry-wide shortage and significant commodity pricing fluctuations.

name: Component Supply Disruption
description: Some products contain certain components for which there are very few qualified suppliers. Extended disruptions at these suppliers could impact the ability to manufacture devices on time to meet consumer demand.

name: Component Supply and Pricing Risk
description: The Company is subject to significant supply and pricing risks from obtaining certain components from single or limited sources, which are susceptible to industry-wide shortages and significant commodity pricing fluctuations that can materially adversely affect the Company's business.

name: Confirmation Order Appeal Risk
description: Certain parties have filed notices of appeal with respect to the Confirmation Order, and there is no assurance that the remaining appeals will not succeed, which could have a material adverse effect on PG&E Corporation and the Utility.

name: Constrained Labor Markets
description: Constrained labor markets increase the company's payroll costs, which can negatively affect operating results.

name: Consumer Lending Regulation Risk
description: PayPal's installment products are subject to federal and state consumer credit and debt collection laws in the U.S., as well as various consumer lending laws in international markets including Japan, Australia, the UK, France, Germany, Spain, and Italy.

name: Consumer Protection Violation Risk
description: Violations of federal and state consumer protection laws, including the Electronic Fund Transfer Act (EFTA) and Regulation E, could result in significant actual damages, statutory damages, penalties, and attorneys' fees.

name: Cost Management Risk
description: Differences between forecast and actual costs can arise due to the volume of work required, market forces affecting labor and materials, and changes in laws and regulations.

name: Cost Recovery Risk
description: The Utility's spending during a period of delay may exceed the authorized amount without an ability to seek cost recovery of such excess, and spending less than authorized could expose the Utility to operational and financial risk.

name: Cost Recovery Uncertainty
description: There is no assurance that the CPUC will authorize the Utility to recover the full amount of costs tracked in memorandum and balancing accounts, including those related to wildfire response, wildfire prevention, third-party wildfire claims, and insurance costs.

name: Cost of Capital Adjustment Risk
description: The approved cost of capital is effective until December 31, 2025, unless the cost of capital adjustment mechanism is triggered, which could impact revenue requirements.

name: Cost of Capital Regulatory Risk
description: Risk related to CPUC decisions on cost of capital adjustments, including the extraordinary event ruling for 2022 that prevented implementation of the cost of capital adjustment mechanism, and uncertainty in future revenue requirements pending outcomes of GRC proceedings.

name: Counterparty Credit Risk
description: Counterparty financial institutions may default on financial or performance obligations, become insolvent, enter receivership, or file for bankruptcy, potentially causing significant losses and limiting the Utility's ability to recover assets.

name: Counterparty Financial Institution Default
description: One or more counterparty financial institutions may default on their financial or performance obligations to PayPal, exposing the company to potential losses.

name: Credit Loss Estimation Uncertainty
description: Estimating current expected credit loss allowances for loans receivable portfolios is inherently uncertain. A deterioration in macroeconomic conditions could result in credit losses that exceed current estimates.

name: Credit Product Funding Risk
description: If PayPal is unable to fund credit products or the purchase of related receivables adequately or in a cost-effective manner, the growth of its credit products could be negatively impacted.

name: Credit Product Risk
description: Credit products expose the company to additional risks, including potential deterioration in merchant and consumer loan receivables driven by changes in acceptable risk parameters.

name: Credit Quality Risk
description: The company faces risk related to the credit quality of outstanding loans, including net charge-off rates and the volume of loans more than 90 days past due across both consumer and merchant portfolios.

name: Credit Rating Downgrade Risk
description: A downgrade of credit ratings, or placement on a watch list for possible downgrading, could increase borrowing rates, including interest rates on borrowings under credit agreements.

name: Credit Rating Risk
description: The company's investment grade credit rating from Standard & Poor's Financial Services may be subject to change, which could affect borrowing costs and access to capital markets.

name: Credit Rating and Liquidity Risk
description: Liquidity, access to capital, and borrowing costs could be adversely impacted by declines in credit rating, financial performance, global credit market conditions, and legal or regulatory proceedings.

name: Credit Risk
description: Fixed-income portfolio diversified with primarily investment-grade securities. Credit default swap contracts used to manage credit exposures relative to broad-based indices and facilitate portfolio diversification.

name: Credit Risk Model Inaccuracy
description: Proprietary risk models may not accurately predict the creditworthiness of a consumer or merchant due to inaccurate assumptions related to the particular consumer or merchant, market conditions, economic environment, or limited transaction history or other data.

name: Credit Risk on Trade Receivables
description: The Company is exposed to credit risk on its trade accounts receivable, vendor non-trade receivables and prepayments related to long-term supply agreements. A substantial majority of outstanding trade receivables are not covered by collateral, third-party bank support, or credit insurance, and risk is heightened during periods when economic conditions worsen.

name: Credit and Financial Instrument Risk
description: Adverse economic conditions can lead to increased credit and collectibility risk on trade receivables, the failure of derivative counterparties and other financial institutions, limitations on the Company's ability to issue new debt, reduced liquidity, and declines in the fair values of the Company's financial instruments.

name: Credit-Risk-Related Contingent Features
description: Counterparty agreements require the company to maintain an investment grade credit rating on its long-term unsecured debt and minimum liquidity of $1.0 billion; failure to meet these requirements would result in posting collateral. As of June 30, 2023, the company's debt was rated AAA and cash investments exceeded $1.0 billion.

name: Critical Accounting Estimates Risk
description: Estimates and assumptions affect reported amounts of assets, liabilities, revenues, and expenses. Changes in accounting estimates are reasonably likely to occur from period to period, and actual results could differ materially from those estimates.

name: Cross-Border Data Transfer Restrictions
description: Restrictions on transferring personal data to countries outside of the EEA, including uncertainty around EU-US bilateral cross-border transfer mechanisms and potential legal challenges.

name: Cross-Border Trade Risk
description: Cross-border trade may be negatively impacted by foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, tariffs, trade barriers or restrictions, sanctions, import or export controls, and the interpretation and application of laws of multiple jurisdictions. Any factors increasing costs or restricting cross-border trade could reduce transactions and volume, negatively impacting revenues and profits.

name: Cryptocurrency Custody Risk
description: Cryptocurrency assets could be treated as a general unsecured claim against the custodian, exposing the company to customer liability for resulting losses.

name: Cryptocurrency Market Volatility
description: Changes in cryptocurrencies, government policies and regulations, new cryptocurrency standards, and shifts in blockchain transaction verification methods have impacted and may continue to impact cryptocurrency mining demand for products.

name: Cryptocurrency Mining Demand Volatility
description: Volatility in cryptocurrency markets, including price changes, government policies, new compute technologies, and changes in blockchain verification methods, creates unpredictable spikes and drops in GPU demand.

name: Cryptocurrency Regulation Risk
description: Current and planned customer cryptocurrency offerings could subject PayPal to additional regulations, licensing requirements, or other obligations. Within the U.S., PayPal is regulated by the New York Department of Financial Services as a virtual currency operator.

name: Cryptocurrency Regulatory Risk
description: The rapidly evolving regulatory landscape for cryptocurrency may subject the company to additional licensing and regulatory obligations, inquiries, or investigations from the SEC or other regulators, and may require product changes, restrictions, or discontinuation of product offerings.

name: Currency Risk
description: The company is exposed to currency risk from euro-denominated debt and foreign exchange rate variability, which is managed using foreign exchange forward contracts designated as cash flow hedging instruments.

name: Custodian Bankruptcy Risk
description: Custodial arrangements to safeguard cryptocurrency assets involve unique risks and uncertainties in the event of the custodian's bankruptcy. Because bankruptcy courts have not yet definitively determined the appropriate treatment of custodial holdings of digital assets, the return of custodied cryptocurrency assets could be delayed or precluded.

name: Custom Component Dependency
description: The Company uses some custom components that are not commonly used by its competitors, and new products often utilize custom components available from only one source, creating supply risks.

name: Customer Arrearage Risk
description: The Utility continues to experience increased customer arrearages due to ongoing economic hardship. Accounts receivable balances over 30 days outstanding were approximately $1.1 billion as of December 31, 2022, approximately $890 million higher than at December 31, 2019.

name: Customer Collection Delays
description: The economic downturn resulting from the COVID-19 pandemic led to reduced customer receipts and collection delays. Accounts receivable balances over 30 days outstanding as of December 31, 2022 were approximately $1.1 billion, roughly $890 million higher compared to December 31, 2019.

name: Customer Compensation Claims Risk
description: If system failures result in damage to customers or their business partners, they could seek significant compensation or contractual penalties, which would be time-consuming and costly to address even if unsuccessful.

name: Customer Concentration Risk
description: The company receives a significant amount of revenue from a limited number of customers. Loss of any large customer, significant reduction in purchases, inability to sell due to trade restrictions, or difficulties collecting accounts receivable would likely harm financial condition and results of operations.

name: Customer Contractual Obligations
description: Risk related to the ability to comply with customers' contractual obligations.

name: Customer Credit Risk
description: Challenging economic conditions may impair the ability of customers to pay for products and services, potentially increasing allowances for doubtful accounts and write-offs of accounts receivable.

name: Customer Data Compromise Risk
description: Because the company is incorporated into the supply chain of a large number of entities worldwide, a compromise of its products or services could affect a significant number of customers and their data, resulting in potential liability and harm to the business.

name: Customer In-House Development Risk
description: Some customers have in-house expertise and internal development capabilities similar to NVIDIA's and can use or develop their own solutions to replace those NVIDIA is providing, including cloud-based services that compete with NVIDIA's AI cloud service offerings.

name: Customer Load Demand Risk
description: The Utility is required to maintain adequate capacity to meet its customers' demand for electricity including peak demand and planning and operating reserves.

name: Customer Protection Program Loss Risk
description: The company faces risk of losses from customer protection programs covering individual consumers, merchants, and transactions, with transaction loss rates ranging between 0.09% and 0.12% of total payment volume.

name: Customer Rate Pressure
description: The company's capital investment plan, increasing procurement of renewable power and energy storage, growing environmental regulations, and cumulative public policy requirements place continuing upward pressure on customer rates.

name: Customer Security Adoption Risk
description: Customers may fail to update their systems, continue running unsupported software, or neglect to install security patches, which could adversely affect the company's reputation and revenue.

name: Customer demand estimation failure
description: Failure to estimate customer demand properly has led and could lead to mismatches between supply and demand.

name: Cyber Incident and Physical Attack Risk
description: The Utility's operational networks and information technology systems could be impacted by a cyber incident, cyber security breach, severe natural event, or physical attack. Cyber-attacks targeting utility systems are increasing in sophistication, magnitude, and frequency, and modernizing infrastructure with new technologies may introduce new cyber security risks.

name: Cyber Liability Insurance Adequacy Risk
description: The Utility maintains cyber liability insurance that covers certain damages caused by cyber incidents. However, there is no assurance that adequate insurance will continue to be available at reasonable rates or that costs of responding to and recovering from a cyber incident will be covered by insurance or recoverable through rates.

name: Cyber Security Risk - Operational Networks
description: Operational networks may face cyber security risks due to modernizing and interconnecting existing infrastructure with new technologies and control systems. Any failure or decrease in functionality could cause harm to the public or employees, significantly disrupt operations, and negatively impact ability to safely generate, transport, deliver and store energy and gas.

name: Cyber and Physical Security Risk
description: FERC is charged with adopting and enforcing mandatory standards governing the reliability of the nation's electric transmission grid, including standards to protect against potential disruptions from cyber and physical security breaches.

name: Cyber-Attacks and Physical Attacks
description: Attacks by third parties including cyber-attacks, acts of terrorism, vandalism, or war impacting operational networks and information technology systems.

name: Cyberattack Impact on Customers
description: Cyberattacks may adversely impact customers even when production services are not directly compromised, and the company may not be able to detect the existence or full extent of attacks affecting all customers.

name: Cyberattacks and Security Vulnerabilities
description: Cyberattacks and security vulnerabilities could result in serious harm to the company's reputation, business, and financial condition.

name: Cybersecurity Legislative Risk
description: Legislative or regulatory action relating to cybersecurity requirements may increase the costs to develop, implement, or secure products and services.

name: Cybersecurity Risk
description: Regulatory scrutiny of cybersecurity practices is increasing globally, with regulatory authorities considering legislative and regulatory proposals that may impose additional obligations on the Company.

name: Cybersecurity Risk from Acquisitions
description: Exposure to additional cybersecurity risks and vulnerabilities as a result of acquisitions or strategic investments.

name: Cybersecurity Threats
description: The Company faces cybersecurity threats including cyberextortion, distributed denial-of-service attacks, ransomware, spear phishing, social engineering schemes, malware, and physical destruction of IT infrastructure, any of which could compromise the confidentiality, availability, and integrity of its data.

name: Cybersecurity Vulnerabilities
description: Security vulnerabilities, data corruption issues, or reduced performance in products and services could harm the company's reputation and lead customers to reduce or delay future purchases.

name: Cybersecurity Workforce Gap
description: Elevated cyberthreat risks are compounded by significant gaps in cybersecurity workforces across 27 countries.

name: Cybersecurity Workforce Shortage
description: The country faces a cybersecurity workforce shortage, with Microsoft targeting to fill half of the shortage by skilling 250,000 people by 2025.

name: Cybersecurity and Data Breaches
description: Product, system security, and data breaches and cyber-attacks could disrupt our operations and adversely affect our financial condition, stock price and reputation.

name: Cybersecurity and Data Protection Risks
description: The company is exposed to product, system security, and data protection breaches, as well as cyber-attacks including ransomware, malware, social-engineering, denial-of-service, and other sophisticated threats that could disrupt operations, reduce revenue, increase expenses, adversely affect stock price, and damage reputation.

name: Cybersecurity and Spam Risk
description: Use of services may be affected by spyware, viruses, phishing and other spam emails, denial of service attacks, data theft, computer intrusions, outages, and similar events.

name: Dam or Hydroelectric Facility Failure Risk
description: The failure of a large dam, major hydroelectric facility, or one or more levees protecting land on which the Utility's assets are built could result in significant property damage, safety risks, and financial liability.

name: Data Breach and Confidentiality Risk
description: Breaches of security measures or unapproved dissemination of proprietary or confidential information could expose the company and partners to risk of loss or misuse, litigation, regulatory actions, and reputational damage.

name: Data Handling Practices Risk
description: Data handling practices across products and services will continue to be under scrutiny. Perceptions of mismanagement driven by regulatory activity or negative public reaction could negatively impact product and feature adoption, product design, and product quality.

name: Data Loss and Security Breach Risk
description: The company collects, processes, stores, and transmits large amounts of confidential, sensitive, proprietary, and personal data, and failure to prevent or mitigate data loss, theft, misuse, or other security breaches could cause significant harm to the business.

name: Data Privacy Policy Misrepresentation Risk
description: If published privacy policies, marketing materials, or other statements are found to be deficient, lacking in transparency, deceptive, unfair, or misrepresentative, the company may be subject to investigation, enforcement actions, or other adverse consequences.

name: Data Privacy and Security Obligations
description: Failure to address or comply with data privacy and security obligations could lead to government enforcement actions, litigation, additional reporting requirements, bans on processing personal data, and orders to destroy or not use personal data, materially adversely affecting reputation, business, or financial condition.

name: Data Protection and Privacy Law Compliance Risk
description: The Company is subject to an increasing number of federal, state, and international laws relating to the collection, use, retention, security, and transfer of various types of personal information. Noncompliance could result in significant penalties or legal liability.

name: Data Security Breach
description: Any systems failure or compromise of security that results in the release of users' data could seriously limit adoption of products and services, harm the company's reputation and brand, and negatively impact the business.

name: Data Security and Fraud Risk
description: The company faces the risk of a breach, compromise, or failure to detect or prevent fraudulent activity involving its data security systems, which could result in liability, fines, higher transaction fees, and loss of ability to process payments.

name: Data Security and Privacy Risk
description: Failure to prevent or mitigate data loss, theft, misuse, or security breaches affecting the company or its vendors' or customers' technology, products, and systems could expose sensitive information and adversely affect operating results. Such failures may also result in litigation, regulatory action, and harm to the company's business and reputation.

name: Datacenter Capacity Constraints
description: Datacenters depend on the availability of permitted and buildable land, predictable energy, networking supplies, and servers including GPUs and other components, posing risk to meeting growing demand for AI services.

name: Debt Collection Risk
description: Risk related to the ability to collect amounts owed when they become due.

name: Debt Covenant Restrictions
description: Material financing agreements contain various covenants restricting the ability to incur indebtedness, assume liens, sell property, merge with other companies, enter sale-leaseback transactions, and enter swap agreements, limiting flexibility in operating the business.

name: Debt and Borrowing Risk
description: The company relies on various credit facilities and debt instruments to fund operations, acquisitions, share repurchases, and capital expenditures, exposing it to refinancing and market condition risks.

name: Declining Natural Gas Demand
description: The Utility expects customer demand for gas to decrease in coming years, primarily in response to policies supporting California's climate goals.

name: Decommissioning Cost Insufficiency
description: To the extent the monies available for decommissioning are insufficient to pay for all reasonable and prudent decommissioning costs, the CPUC must authorize the electric utility to collect these charges from its customers.

name: Deferred Tax Asset Realization Risk
description: Valuation allowance related to capital loss carryforwards, state, and certain other deferred tax assets that management determined not likely to be realized due to jurisdictional projections of future taxable income, including capital gains.

name: Deferred Tax Asset Recoverability Risk
description: There is a risk that deferred tax assets may not be fully realized, potentially requiring a valuation allowance to be recorded against future tax deductions and credits.

name: Deferred Tax Asset Risk
description: Actual and forecasted earnings are subject to change due to economic, political, and other conditions, and significant judgment is required in determining the ability to utilize deferred tax assets.

name: Delayed GRC Decisions
description: Delayed decisions in General Rate Case proceedings may require the Utility to develop budgets based on approved revenue requirements and estimated outcomes rather than authorized amounts, potentially exposing the Utility to operational and financial risk.

name: Demand Estimation Risk
description: Failure to estimate customer demand properly has led and could lead to mismatches between supply and demand, resulting in product shortages and excess inventory, significantly harming financial results.

name: Demand Forecasting Risk
description: Risk of underestimating or overestimating customer demand for products, leading to inventory issues, lost revenue, damaged customer relationships, and impacted gross margins.

name: Dependence on Partner Network
description: The company relies heavily on a broad partner network including OEMs, ODMs, system builders, AIBs, retailers, distributors, ISVs, CSPs, automotive manufacturers, and tier-1 automotive suppliers to reach end customers and markets, creating dependency risk.

name: Dependence on Transcending Current Product Categories
description: Future growth depends on the ability to transcend current product category definitions, business models, and sales motions.

name: Design and Manufacturing Defects
description: The Company's products and services may be affected by design and manufacturing defects in hardware, software, or third-party components, which could materially adversely affect the Company's business and harm its reputation.

name: Device Market Demand Variability
description: Differences in device market demand between developed markets and growth markets impact Windows OEM revenue.

name: Diablo Canyon Early Closure Risk
description: The Utility may incur a material charge if it ceases operations at Diablo Canyon's two nuclear generation units before their respective current licenses expire in 2024 and 2025. As of December 31, 2022, the unrecovered investment in Diablo Canyon was $840 million.

name: Diablo Canyon Extended Operations Risk
description: If Diablo Canyon enters extended operations through no later than 2030, the Utility will face operational challenges from a shortened planning period, including potential inability to procure adequate nuclear fuel supply.

name: Diablo Canyon Extension Cost Risk
description: Extended operations at Diablo Canyon through no later than 2030 involve significant costs, with customers of CPUC-jurisdictional load-serving entities responsible for bearing those costs.

name: Diablo Canyon License Expiration
description: The NRC operating licenses for Diablo Canyon Units 1 and 2 currently expire in 2024 and 2025 respectively, posing a risk to continued nuclear generation operations.

name: Digital Content Availability Risk
description: Failure to obtain or create digital content that appeals to customers, or to make such content available on commercially reasonable terms, could have a material adverse impact on the Company's business, results of operations and financial condition.

name: Digital Content Production Risk
description: The Company produces its own digital content, which can be costly due to intense competition for talent, content and subscribers, and may fail to appeal to customers.

name: Digital Rights Management Risk
description: Breach or malfunctioning of digital rights management technology could subject the company to claims, and content providers may be unwilling to include their content in the service.

name: Digital Safety Abuse Risk
description: Hosted consumer and enterprise services may be used to generate or disseminate harmful or illegal content, leading to regulatory oversight, civil or criminal liability, or reputational damage.

name: Dilution Risk
description: The Company will continue to rely on equity sales of common shares to fund business operations, and issuances of additional shares will result in dilution to existing stockholders.

name: Dilution from equity or convertible debt securities
description: The sale of additional equity or convertible debt securities would be dilutive to existing shareholders.

name: Dilutive Equity Issuance Risk
description: The potential for acquisitions to result in dilutive issuances of equity securities.

name: Disaster Recovery Planning Insufficiency
description: Disaster recovery planning and testing may not be sufficient for all possible outcomes or events, and regulators may require specific business continuity and disaster recovery plans that are costly and time-consuming to implement.

name: Disclosure and Misuse of Personal Data
description: As cloud-based offerings grow, increasing amounts of personal data are stored and processed, and improper disclosure or misuse could harm the company's reputation, create legal exposure, or subject it to liability under data protection laws, resulting in increased costs or loss of revenue.

name: Disruptive Technological Changes
description: Rapid, significant, and disruptive technological changes impact the industries in which the company operates, including payment technologies, AI, machine learning, and others. New technologies may be superior to or render obsolete the technologies currently used.

name: Distributed Energy Resources Adaptation Risk
description: The Utility may be unable to effectively adapt to potential business and regulatory changes related to distributed energy resources such as on-site solar generation, electric vehicles, energy storage, fuel cells, energy efficiency, and demand response technologies, impacting its business model and ability to execute on its strategy.

name: Distributed Generation and Energy Storage Competition
description: Technology-related cost declines and sustained federal or state subsidies could make distributed generation and energy storage a viable alternative to the Utility's bundled electric service, further reducing energy demand.

name: Dividend Payment Restrictions
description: The ability of the Utility to pay dividends or make other advances, distributions, and transfers of funds depends on its results of operations and may be restricted by applicable laws limiting the amount of funds available for payment of dividends and certain restrictive covenants.

name: Dividend Restriction Risk
description: Per the Confirmation Order, PG&E Corporation may not pay dividends on its common stock until it recognizes $6.2 billion in Non-GAAP Core Earnings following the Emergence Date.

name: Downward Pressure on Operating Margin
description: Margins could experience downward pressure due to increasing competition, increased costs, higher percentage of revenues from Google Network Members, lower margins on digital content/apps, mobile advertising, newer advertising formats, and increased R&D investment.

name: Drought and Generation Risk
description: Drought conditions challenged the Utility's generation operations, requiring rescheduling of outages to maximize availability during peak summer demand periods.

name: Drought and Vegetation Management Costs
description: Severe drought conditions in California directed the Utility to perform additional fire prevention and vegetation management work, with associated costs tracked in California Energy Management Assistance accounts and later in a Vegetation Management Balancing Account.

name: Due Diligence Failure Risk
description: Potential failure of due diligence processes to identify significant issues with the assets or company in which the company is investing or acquiring.

name: E-commerce Reporting Obligations
description: U.S. states, the U.S. federal government, and foreign jurisdictions have implemented reporting or record-keeping obligations on companies that engage in or facilitate e-commerce. Failure to comply could result in substantial monetary penalties and other sanctions.

name: ECB Significant Supervised Entity Risk
description: If the business activities of PayPal (Europe) exceed certain thresholds, or if the ECB so determines, PayPal (Europe) may be deemed a significant supervised entity and certain activities would become directly supervised by the ECB, increasing compliance costs.

name: EEA and UK Credit Institution Regulation Risk
description: PayPal (Europe), licensed as a credit institution in Luxembourg, may be subject to enforcement actions and significant fines if it violates applicable requirements under EEA and UK regulations.

name: ERP System Implementation Risk
description: ERP system problems upon implementation, such as quality issues or programming errors, could impact continued ability to operate or timely report financial results.

name: ESG Compliance Risk
description: The Company faces risks related to environmental, social, and governance considerations, including climate change, greenhouse gas emissions, human and civil rights, and diversity, equity, and inclusion. Failure to achieve ESG goals could result in legal and regulatory proceedings and materially adversely affect the Company's business, reputation, results of operations, financial condition, and stock price.

name: ESG Issues
description: Environmental, social, and governance (ESG) issues may adversely affect PayPal's business, financial condition, and results of operations, and may damage its reputation. Failure to adapt to evolving ESG laws, regulations, or stakeholder expectations could further harm the company.

name: ESG Reporting and Compliance Risk
description: Governments around the world are adopting laws and regulations pertaining to ESG performance, transparency, and reporting, including the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive and climate-related risk management requirements.

name: ESG Risks
description: The company faces risks and uncertainties related to Environmental, Social, and Corporate Governance practices, including potential impacts from climate change regulations and related market effects.

name: ESG and Sustainability Risk
description: Increased scrutiny from shareholders, regulators and others regarding environmental, social and governance responsibilities could result in additional costs or risks and adversely impact reputation and willingness of customers and suppliers to do business with the company.

name: ESG scrutiny
description: Increased scrutiny from shareholders, regulators, and others regarding our environmental, social and governance responsibilities could result in financial, reputational and operational harm.

name: EU Digital Legislation Risk
description: New EU requirements, including the Digital Markets Act, Digital Services Act, and Data Act, impose additional rules and restrictions on the use of data in products and services, increasing compliance costs and engineering burden.

name: EU Whistleblower Directive Compliance Risk
description: The EU Whistleblower Directive, initiated in 2021, may present compliance challenges to the extent it is implemented in different forms by EU member states.

name: EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework Risk
description: The EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework is subject to further legal challenge, which could create uncertainty around the legal requirements for data transfers from the EU. EU data protection authorities may also block the use of certain U.S.-based services.

name: Economic Sanctions Impact
description: Increasing use of economic sanctions may impact demand for products or services, negatively impacting business and financial results.

name: Effective Tax Rate Variability
description: Future effective tax rate may be affected by changes in business or statutory rates, mix of earnings across countries, available tax incentives, credits and deductions, expiration of statute of limitations, settlements of tax audits, accounting principles changes, stock-based compensation tax effects, and volatility in stock price.

name: Effective Tax Rate Volatility
description: The effective tax rate is subject to significant variation due to factors including variability in pre-tax and taxable income and loss, mix of jurisdictions, intercompany transactions, acquisitions, investments, changes in statutes, regulations, and other laws in various jurisdictions.

name: Electric Asset Inspection Failures
description: The Utility has notified the CPUC of various errors relating to inspections and maintenance of its electric assets or implementation of WMP initiatives, including missed inspections and inability to locate records evidencing performance of inspections.

name: Electric Load Electrification Uncertainty
description: The Utility expects customer electric load to increase due to electrification of buildings and transportation, but cannot predict how quickly this will occur.

name: Electric System Reliability Risk
description: Potential for system RA shortages beginning in 2021, requiring incremental procurement of system-level qualifying RA capacity of 3,300 MWs by all LSEs operating within the CAISO's balancing area for the period from 2021 to 2023.

name: Electric and Gas Industry Change Risk
description: Industry changes, costs associated with complying with new regulatory developments and initiatives and with technological advancements, or the Utility's inability to successfully adapt to changes in the electric and gas industry, could materially affect the Utility's financial condition, results of operations, liquidity, and cash flows.

name: Electricity Procurement Cost Disallowance Risk
description: The CPUC may disallow costs associated with electricity purchases if the costs were not incurred in compliance with the CPUC-approved plan or if the CPUC determines that the utility failed to follow the principles of least-cost dispatch.

name: Electricity and Natural Gas Operations Hazard Risk
description: The Utility's electricity and natural gas operations are inherently hazardous and involve significant risks, including breakdown of equipment, explosions, fires, and large-scale system disruptions.

name: Eminent Domain Risk
description: Jurisdictions may attempt to acquire the Utility's assets through eminent domain, causing the Utility to lose customers in the municipality.

name: Employee Turnover Risk
description: PG&E Corporation experienced significant employee turnover throughout the course of its Chapter 11 Cases in 2019 and 2020, particularly at the executive management level.

name: Energy Procurement Credit Risk
description: The Utility faces credit risk exposure to wholesale customers and counterparties in energy procurement, with total exposure of $814 million as of December 31, 2022, compared to $570 million as of December 31, 2021.

name: Energy Storage Procurement Obligations
description: The Utility is required to procure 580 MW of qualifying storage capacity by end of 2020 with all projects operational by end of 2024 under the CPUC multi-year energy storage procurement framework.

name: Enforcement and Investigation Risk
description: The Utility is the target of a number of investigations, and if those investigations result in enforcement action, the Utility could incur substantial additional fines or penalties and suffer further ongoing negative consequences.

name: Enforcement and Legal Matter Risk
description: PG&E Corporation and the Utility have recorded material accruals related to various enforcement and legal matters.

name: Enforcement and Litigation Risk
description: PG&E Corporation and the Utility are subject to various laws and regulations and are named as parties in a number of claims and lawsuits. Penalties may be incurred for failure to comply with federal, state, or local laws and regulations.

name: Enforcement and Regulatory Risk
description: The Utility faces risks related to the Enhanced Oversight and Enforcement Process, legislative and regulatory developments, and the outcomes of enforcement, regulatory, and ratemaking proceedings.

name: Enforcement, Litigation, and Regulatory Matters
description: The Utility is subject to various enforcement, litigation, and regulatory matters—including Safety Culture OII, EOEP proceedings, WMP actions, and safety self-reports—which could result in penalties, additional regulatory requirements, or changes to operations.

name: Enhanced Oversight and Enforcement Process (EOEP)
description: The CPUC adopted an Enhanced Oversight and Enforcement Process (EOEP) to monitor the Utility's operational performance, with trigger events including failure to obtain an approved Wildfire Mitigation Plan, failure to comply with regulatory reporting requirements, insufficient progress toward approved safety or risk-driven investments, and failure to meet certain metrics. As a result, the Utility may face fines, penalties, or other regulatory actions.

name: Environmental Laws and Remediation Costs
description: The Utility's operations are subject to extensive environmental laws that are subject to change, and the Utility has been required to pay for environmental remediation costs at various sites and may be required to do so in the future.

name: Environmental Liabilities
description: Unanticipated costs associated with environmental liabilities could affect results of operations.

name: Environmental Regulation
description: The Utility's operations are subject to extensive federal, state, and local environmental, health, and safety laws and permits, covering areas such as hazardous substance remediation, greenhouse gas emissions reporting, pollutant discharge, and nuclear fuel handling.

name: Environmental Regulatory Risk
description: Environmental regulations governing air quality, water quality and usage, remediation of hazardous substances, and protection of natural resources and wildlife pose compliance risks. California's SB 100 increased the required share of renewable energy in the state's electricity portfolio to 60% by 2030, adding further regulatory obligations.

name: Environmental Remediation Liabilities
description: The Utility is subject to loss contingencies under federal and California environmental laws that may require it to pay for environmental remediation at sites where it is a potentially responsible party, including former MGP sites, power plant sites, gas compressor stations, and hazardous material storage and disposal sites.

name: Environmental Risk
description: The Utility faces risks related to severe weather conditions, extended drought, and climate change, including resulting events such as wildfires, as well as compliance with extensive environmental laws.

name: Environmental, Social and Governance Disclosure Risk
description: New environmental, social, and governance laws and regulations are expanding mandatory disclosure, reporting, and diligence requirements.

name: Equity Investment Risk
description: Strategic investments are subject to market-related risks that could substantially reduce or increase the carrying value of the portfolio. Strategic investments totaled $2.1 billion and $3.2 billion as of December 31, 2022 and 2021, representing approximately 14% and 20% of the total cash, cash equivalents, and short-term and long-term investment portfolio, respectively.

name: Equity Issuance Risk
description: PG&E Corporation's planned equity issuances may be affected by factors including liquidity and cash flow needs, capital expenditures, interest rates, the timing and outcome of ratemaking proceedings, and the timing and terms of other financings, including the potential sale of a minority interest in Pacific Generation.

name: Equity Market Price Risk
description: Securities held in the equity investments portfolio are subject to market price risk, which is managed at times using options, futures, and swap contracts not designated as hedging instruments.

name: Equity Securities Ownership Restriction
description: Any person acquiring PG&E Corporation Equity Securities will be restricted from owning 4.75% or more of such Equity Securities as determined for federal income tax purposes, subject to certain exceptions.

name: Estimates and Assumptions Risk
description: Preparing financial statements requires management to make estimates and assumptions that affect reported amounts of assets, liabilities, revenue, and expenses. Actual results and outcomes may differ from management's estimates and assumptions due to risks and uncertainties.

name: Ethereum 2.0 Merge Impact
description: Changes to cryptocurrency standards including the Ethereum 2.0 merge may decrease the usage of GPUs for Ethereum mining and create increased aftermarket sales of GPUs, which could negatively impact retail prices and reduce demand for new GPUs.

name: European Regulatory Risk
description: Regulators in EEA countries could enforce local consumer protection laws, impose additional obligations and costs, and impact the company's ability to expand its business in Europe and the UK.

name: Evolving Cyberthreats
description: Cyberthreats are constantly evolving and becoming increasingly sophisticated, making detection and defense more difficult. Breaches could disrupt business applications and impair the company's ability to provide services.

name: Evolving Data Protection Laws
description: Data protection laws around the world are quickly changing and may be interpreted in an increasingly stringent fashion inconsistent with current data practices, necessitating changes to information technologies, systems, and practices.

name: Evolving IT Environment and Regulatory Risks
description: The company's internal IT environment continues to evolve with new devices and technologies, and business policies and internal security controls may not keep pace with emerging threats or cybersecurity regulations across jurisdictions worldwide.

name: Evolving Regulatory Environment Risk
description: The legal and regulatory environment relating to privacy and data protection laws continues to develop and evolve in unpredictable ways, including with respect to technologies such as cloud computing, artificial intelligence, machine learning, cryptocurrency, and blockchain technology.

name: Excess or Obsolete Inventory
description: Changes in business and economic conditions, market conditions, sudden decreases in demand, inventory obsolescence due to changing technology and customer requirements, new product introductions, failure to estimate customer demand properly, or ordering in advance of historical lead-times may result in excess or obsolete inventory or excess product purchase commitments.

name: Executive Dependency and Succession Planning
description: Highly dependent on longstanding executive team; failure in succession planning, knowledge transfer, and smooth transitions could hinder strategic planning and long-term success.
